steps.title: Identify what's missing from Grasby et al.'s current data
steps.content: The research question asks whether “perceived category similarity” predicts consumers' likelihood of purchasing brand extensions. Grasby et al. have already calculated purchase probability across categories; what they need is perceived similarity between the categories themselves.

steps.title: Find the design that measures category similarity
steps.content: Choice C measures households' perception of how similar the product CATEGORIES in each pair are (e.g., cooking oil vs. butter) and correlates those ratings with Grasby's probability change data. A measures within-category product similarity, while B and D address brand recognition and cost—concepts unrelated to category similarity. That matches choice C — C is correct.
